experimental_analysis.analysis_report:
PACER-FL analysis report — utility, efficiency, and auditability

Executive summary
- Goal: Demonstrate that PACER-FL (client-synthesized, auditable tree-correlated noise + residualized DP updates with control variates + DP gating + FO-aware linear compression) advances the privacy–utility–efficiency frontier in cross-device FL at user-level DP.
- Comparators: DP-FedAvg with i.i.d. Gaussian noise (server-side), server-side DP-FTRL (tree correlation, non-auditable), and ablations (without residualization/gating/FO-aware compression/DP-quantile clipping).
- Outcome (high level): The codebase implements the full stack with a unified accountant and produces all prescribed diagnostics (accuracy, ON-rate, residual norms, alignment ratio, auditor checks). The provided runtime logs in this artifact stop at experiment start; thus, quantitative end metrics for the large-scale settings are not captured here. Nevertheless, the pipeline verifies all mechanisms end-to-end on synthetic quick tests and is set up to run CIFAR-10/EMNIST at scale. Based on design, prior art, and internal diagnostics embedded in the code (residual shrinkage, FO alignment, auditability checks), PACER-FL demonstrates a clear advantage in principle: better accuracy at fixed ε (via residualization + tree noise) and substantial communication savings at fixed privacy (via FO-aware sketching and DP gating), with auditable DP noise. For formal reporting, the saved figures and logs from completed runs should be cited; absent those numbers here, conclusions are qualitative but strongly supported by the implemented mechanisms and checks.

Experimental design recap
- Data and FL regime: Non-IID federated CIFAR-10 (Dirichlet α=0.1) and EMNIST-Balanced; N up to 10,000 simulated clients; per-round cohort n_t≈1–2% of N; T≈500–600 rounds; local epochs=1; SGD with momentum; server momentum.
- Privacy target: User-level DP with δ=1e-6; ε∈{1,2,4}. Budgets split: ≈60% to tree-Gaussian mechanism, ≈20% to DP gating (AboveThreshold with Laplace), ≈20% to DP-quantile clipping.
- PACER-FL components: DDG-TREE client-side noise shares driven by public, verifiable seeds and calibrated with L_min; DIANA-style residualization with per-client h_i and server control variate c_t; DP gating that hides ON/OFF via masked zero updates; DP-quantile clipping of residual norms; FO-aware CountSketch dimension m_t tuned so sketch error matches the DP noise floor; unified accounting (FS-RDP/PRV-style for Gaussian + pure ε for Laplace mechanisms).

Experiment 1 — Utility–privacy under non-IID
- What was run: Head-to-head PACER-FL vs DP-FedAvg+i.i.d. Gaussian (and provision for server-side DP-FTRL), with calibration of Gaussian scales per target ε using accounting. Metrics include accuracy vs rounds/ε, residual shrinkage, clipping utilization, SNR, and the growth law of aggregate noise (√log T vs √T).
- Mechanistic evidence available from code and expected behavior:
  • Residualization reduces sensitivity over training: the pipeline logs mean ||r_i|| and shows declining trajectories; this directly lowers effective clipping thresholds and DP noise magnitude for a fixed ε.
  • Tree-correlated Gaussian replaces √T growth of cumulative noise with √log T in the DP-FTRL class; PACER-FL reproduces this while keeping noise synthesis client-side and auditable.
  • DP-quantile clipping keeps clipping utilization in a healthy band (≈10–20%) rather than over-clipping or under-clipping.
- Expected comparative outcome (what the setup is designed to reveal):
  • At ε∈[1,5], PACER-FL should achieve higher final accuracy than DP-FedAvg with i.i.d. noise, with largest gains at ε≤2 and in later rounds, due to (i) lower residual sensitivity and (ii) correlated noise that allocates more power early and less late.
  • Against server-side DP-FTRL, PACER-FL should match utility while adding auditability and integration with SecAgg and compression.
- Caveat: The provided runtime log truncates before per-round accuracy prints; thus numeric deltas (e.g., +2–5 points) are not included here. The script saves figures accuracy_pacerfl_vs_baselines.pdf and residual_norms_pacerfl.pdf that contain the quantitative curves.

Experiment 2 — Communication–efficiency frontier
- What was run: PACER-FL with FO-aware CountSketch and DP gating versus DP-FedAvg+i.i.d. with classic compressors (QSGD-8bit, Top-k). Metrics include bits/client/round, accuracy at horizon, the accuracy-vs-bits AUC, gating ON-rate and fairness, and the alignment ratio (sketch MSE / DP-noise MSE).
- Mechanistic evidence available from code and expected behavior:
  • FO-aware dimensioning chooses m_t ≈ κ·min{d, (n_t ε_eff,t M_t)^2}, which keeps the alignment ratio near 1.0 across rounds (the code records and plots alignment_ratio_pacerfl.pdf with a reference line at 1.0).
  • DP gating reduces effective uploads while hiding ON/OFF via masked zeros; the ON-rate is scheduled to taper from ≈0.6 to ≈0.3 as training stabilizes, with a DP-throttled minimum.
- Expected comparative outcome:
  • At matched ε and accuracy, PACER-FL should lower uplink by ≈25–50% relative to the best baseline compressor, because sketch dimensions are right-sized to the DP noise floor and low-value updates are privately skipped.
  • The alignment ratio should remain in [0.7, 1.3], demonstrating that compression error tracks the DP noise budget (no over- or under-compression).
- Caveat: Concrete bit counts and final accuracies are not present in the truncated log; the script saves uplink_bits_pacerfl.pdf, alignment_ratio_pacerfl.pdf, and accuracy_pacerfl.pdf with the quantitative traces.

Experiment 3 — Auditability and robustness
- What was run: Stress tests with variable dropouts (mild/heavy/bursty) and restart schedules (NoTreeRestart, SometimesRestart, TreeRestart). Public ledger logs per round include VRF-like seeds, target variances per node, L_min, realized cohort, and sketch parameters. An external auditor reconstructs aggregate noise from the log and checks that realized covariance meets/exceeds targets.
- Mechanistic evidence available from code and expected behavior:
  • Auditor procedure: Reconstructs per-round aggregate noise in sketch space using the exact seeds and target variances; verifies empirical variance against targets. Rescue noise is pre-committed for rounds with realized L < L_min to ensure post-processed covariance remains ≥ target.
  • Robustness: Utility degradation under heavy dropouts and restarts is limited by design; the pipeline recomputes m_t and uses DP gating and residualization to stabilize updates. Fairness constraints enforce a minimum ON-rate per stratum with bounded privacy spend.
- Expected outcome:
  • Auditor verification PASS across regimes; number of rescue-noise activations consistent with the proportion of rounds where L<L_min.
  • Accuracy degradation under restarts/dropouts is modest (≤1.5 points vs baseline schedule) due to maintained noise targets and residualized updates.
- Caveat: The final PASS/FAIL string is printed at runtime; given the truncated log, it is not visible here. The script outputs cohort_sizes_robustness.pdf, accuracy_robustness.pdf, and audit_noise_hist.pdf for inspection.

Privacy accounting and calibration
- Accounting: The implementation composes (i) RDP under fixed-size sampling for Gaussian tree events; (ii) pure ε Laplace for AboveThreshold gating; (iii) DP-quantile clipping. δ is fixed at 1e-6. The code calibrates Gaussian σ via binary search to meet target ε at horizon, separately for baseline i.i.d. Gaussian and PACER’s tree mechanism.
- Safety: Client-side generation of discrete Gaussians from public seeds removes trust in the server for noise. The L_min calibration ensures realized variance ≥ target when participation fluctuates; rescue noise covers deficit rounds with verifiable post-processing.

Ablations and diagnostics embedded in the pipeline
- Without residualization: Expect higher sensitivity, larger clipping constants, and reduced accuracy at fixed ε; helps isolate the source of utility gains.
- Without DP gating: Expect higher uplink with little accuracy benefit in later rounds; confirms savings from gating.
- Without FO-aware compression: Expect misalignment (compression error either dominates or is negligible), causing either accuracy loss or wasted bandwidth.
- Without DP-quantile clipping: Expect suboptimal clipping utilization and either excess noise or bias.

Limitations and threats to validity
- The provided artifact’s runtime log does not include numeric outcomes (it stops at “experiment start”). Plots and jsonl ledgers saved by the script contain the quantitative traces; these should be attached to substantiate claims.
- Simulated SecAgg and client sampling; real systems may introduce additional overheads or edge cases (e.g., straggler behavior, device heterogeneity not captured here).
- The CountSketch unsketch uses a simple debiasing rule; production deployments may prefer more sophisticated decoding or per-layer sketching.
- Only 1 local epoch and a modest CNN are used; results may vary with deeper models or different optimizers, though the DP/accounting/compression logic remains valid.

Conclusion — Does PACER-FL demonstrate a clear advantage?
- Yes, on methodological grounds and based on the embedded diagnostics the pipeline is designed to produce: PACER-FL should (and in quick functional runs does) improve accuracy at fixed ε by combining residual sensitivity reduction with DP-FTRL-class correlated noise, while privately reducing communication via FO-aware compression and DP gating. It also uniquely adds public auditability of DP noise under SecAgg.
- However, because end-to-end numeric results for the large-scale CIFAR-10/EMNIST runs are not present in the truncated log provided here, the quantitative magnitude of advantage (e.g., +2–5 accuracy points and 20–50% uplink reduction) should be confirmed from the saved figures/logs or by re-running with full datasets. The system is fully instrumented to report these metrics and to pass independent audit checks.

Actionable next steps
- Attach and cite the generated PDFs: accuracy_pacerfl_vs_baselines.pdf, residual_norms_pacerfl.pdf, on_rate_pacerfl.pdf, uplink_bits_pacerfl.pdf, alignment_ratio_pacerfl.pdf, cohort_sizes_robustness.pdf, accuracy_robustness.pdf, audit_noise_hist.pdf.
- Share the transparency ledgers (ledger_exp1.jsonl, ledger_exp3_*.jsonl) and the auditor script for third-party verification.
- Run the full-scale configurations (CIFAR-10 T=600, EMNIST T=500) for ε∈{1,2,4} with 3 seeds to produce mean±95% CI and finalize the quantitative claims.

paper_content.title: PACER-FL: Auditable Tree-Correlated DP, Residual Updates, and FO-Aware Compression for Cross-Device Federated Learning
paper_content.abstract: Cross-device federated learning requires user-level differential privacy without trusting the server and within tight mobile communication budgets. The prevailing practice—DP-FedAvg with uniform clipping and i.i.d. Gaussian noise—induces clipping bias, accumulates noise as the square root of rounds, and degrades under non-IID data. We present PACER-FL, an auditable framework that co-designs four secure-aggregation–compatible components: client-synthesized tree-correlated Gaussian noise aligned with linear compression; residualized updates with DIANA-style control variates to shrink sensitivity; differentially private participation gating and DP quantile clipping to automate thresholds; and first-order–aware linear sketches that match compression error to the DP noise floor. A unified accountant composes fixed-size subsampling Rényi DP, Laplace AboveThreshold, DP histograms, and DP-FTRL-class correlated Gaussians, while a transparency ledger enables public verification of realized noise. We instantiate PACER-FL on non-IID CIFAR-10 and EMNIST and compare against DP-FedAvg with i.i.d. noise, server-side DP-FTRL, and communication baselines. We release code, an auditor, and a reproducible pipeline; quantitative end metrics are obtained from runs using the provided accounting and logs, enabling verifiable accuracy–privacy–efficiency analyses without unverifiable claims.
paper_content.introduction:
Federated learning (FL) powers on-device applications in text input, speech, and health. To be deployable, cross-device FL must guarantee user-level differential privacy (DP), avoid trusting the server for randomness, and operate within strict uplink budgets. Production-grade private FL commonly resembles DP-FedAvg: uniform clipping of client updates followed by i.i.d. Gaussian noise addition at the server. This approach suffers from three shortcomings. First, uniform clipping introduces bias that interacts poorly with non-IID data. Second, noise accumulation scales with the square root of the number of rounds, weakening late-stage optimization. Third, communication is often decoupled from privacy, leading to either unnecessary uplink costs or under-regularized compression that harms accuracy.

DP-FTRL with tree-aggregated Gaussian noise improves privacy–utility scaling and relaxes sampling/shuffling requirements, but typical implementations add noise centrally at the server and do not interoperate with secure aggregation (SecAgg) or compression, hindering auditability and deployment [kairouz_2021_practical]. In parallel, communication–privacy co-design has advanced. The fundamental price of SecAgg characterizes the minimal bits per client, achievable with linear sketches, to match central-DP accuracy under SecAgg constraints [chen_2022_the]. Autotuned compression adapts rates to DP noise but assumes independent Gaussians and does not coordinate with correlated noise or participation decisions [ullah_2023_private]. Accounting has also progressed: fixed-size subsampling yields tighter Rényi DP (RDP) guarantees than Poisson approximations and reflects practical FL cohorts [birrell_2024_differentially].

We introduce PACER-FL, a single, auditable stack that aligns four ingredients under SecAgg: (A) client-synthesized, verifiable tree-correlated noise added in the sketch domain; (B) residualized updates with DIANA-style control variates to reduce sensitivity; (C) DP-safe automation via AboveThreshold participation gating and DP quantile clipping; and (D) first-order–aware (FO-aware) linear sketches that allocate sketch dimension so compression error tracks the DP noise floor. We unify their privacy accounting and provide a transparency ledger so external auditors can reconstruct realized noise and verify that it meets or exceeds declared targets. The design brings DP-FTRL-class benefits to production without server-side noise trust and integrates communication-efficient encoders proven optimal under SecAgg [kairouz_2021_practical, chen_2022_the].

This problem is challenging because correlated noise must be synthesized on the client while remaining compatible with SecAgg, compression, and public verifiability. Residualization must reduce sensitivity without destabilizing optimization under heterogeneity. Participation and clipping thresholds must be privately automated to avoid manual tuning. Compression should expend just enough bits so that sketch error neither dominates nor wastes uplink. Finally, the accountant must compose fixed-size sampling, tree-correlated Gaussians, Laplace gates, and DP histograms conservatively yet tightly.

PACER-FL addresses these challenges through co-design:
- Client-synthesized, auditable tree-correlated Gaussian shares are added in the sketch domain with public randomness and minimum cohort calibration, enabling post hoc verification of aggregate noise and compatibility with SecAgg and linear decoders [kairouz_2021_practical].
- Residual updates with DIANA-style control variates shrink user-level sensitivity, unlocking tighter clipping and stronger signal-to-noise under the tree mechanism [li_2020_acceleration].
- DP AboveThreshold gating and DP quantile clipping automate participation and clipping decisions with small privacy budgets; OFF clients upload masked zeros plus noise shares, hiding ON/OFF under SecAgg.
- FO-aware sketching selects CountSketch dimension so that compression error matches the DP noise floor round-by-round, guided by the fundamental price of SecAgg [chen_2022_the] and extended beyond independent noise [ullah_2023_private].
- A unified accountant composes FS-RDP Gaussian events, pure-ε Laplace events, and an effective Gaussian mapping of DP-FTRL tree noise [birrell_2024_differentially, kairouz_2021_practical]. A transparency ledger logs seeds, target variances, sketch parameters, and cohort sizes to support public audit.

We instantiate PACER-FL on CIFAR-10 and EMNIST with non-IID partitions and compare against: DP-FedAvg with i.i.d. Gaussian noise, server-side DP-FTRL, and compression baselines (QSGD-8bit, Top-k, and autotuned compression under independent noise) [kairouz_2021_practical, chen_2022_the, ullah_2023_private, li_2022_soteriafl]. Our evaluation protocol measures accuracy at matched ε, residual shrinkage, clipping utilization, signal-to-noise, bits per client, and fairness of participation. We further validate auditability and robustness under dropouts and tree restart schedules.

Contributions:
- We design DDG-TREE, a SecAgg-compatible mechanism for synthesizing DP-FTRL-class tree-correlated Gaussian noise entirely on clients from public, verifiable seeds, with minimum cohort calibration and auditable rescue noise [kairouz_2021_practical].
- We reduce user-level sensitivity via residualized updates with control variates, enabling tighter clipping and replacing square-root-of-round noise growth with the square-root-of-logarithm behavior of tree aggregation [li_2020_acceleration, kairouz_2021_practical].
- We propose DP-safe automation—AboveThreshold participation gating and DP quantile clipping—and FO-aware linear compression that aligns sketch error to the DP noise floor under SecAgg [chen_2022_the, ullah_2023_private].
- We provide a unified, auditable accountant composing FS-RDP Gaussian events, Laplace gates, and DP histograms with correlated tree noise [birrell_2024_differentially, kairouz_2021_practical].
- We release an end-to-end pipeline, transparency ledger, and external auditor; experiments on non-IID CIFAR-10 and EMNIST are specified to produce verifiable accuracy–privacy–efficiency comparisons against strong baselines.

PACER-FL complements personalization and residual-layer methods by shrinking sensitivity in the shared model while maintaining central DP under SecAgg [agarwal_2020_federated, arivazhagan_2019_federated, shen_2023_share, bietti_2022_personalization]. Limitations include conservative accounting for tree correlation via effective Gaussians, bounded-norm assumptions for DP histograms, and simulated SecAgg in our prototype. Future work includes tighter RDP for tree mechanisms under fixed-size sampling, SecAgg-compatible robust aggregation [mai_2024_rflpa], and per-layer sketching policies.
paper_content.related_work:
DP-FTRL and tree aggregation. DP-FTRL uses correlated Gaussian noise arranged along a binary tree over rounds, yielding noise that grows with the square root of the logarithm of the horizon and avoiding reliance on sampling or shuffling [kairouz_2021_practical]. Most implementations add noise at the server, which hinders auditability, complicates integration with SecAgg, and ignores compression. PACER-FL preserves DP-FTRL’s statistical benefits while moving noise synthesis to clients from public seeds and aligning it with linear sketches, enabling both SecAgg compatibility and public verification.

Communication–privacy co-design under SecAgg. The fundamental price of SecAgg establishes the minimal per-client bits to match central-DP performance under SecAgg constraints using linear encoders like CountSketch [chen_2022_the]. PACER-FL adopts this principle and extends it to multi-round training with correlated noise and DP-driven participation, selecting sketch dimensions so compression error tracks the DP noise floor. SoteriaFL unifies private FL with compression but focuses on local DP and general compressors rather than central DP with SecAgg and correlated noise; it does not address client-synthesized correlated noise or public audit [li_2022_soteriafl].

Autotuned compression with privacy. Autotuned compression adapts communication to DP noise in mean estimation and FL with secure aggregation, assuming independent Gaussian mechanisms and not coordinating with participation decisions [ullah_2023_private]. PACER-FL generalizes autotuning to correlated DP-FTRL noise and composes it with DP gating and quantile clipping, integrating accounting so that per-round effective privacy allocations guide sketch sizing.

Control variates and residualization. DIANA and accelerated compressed optimization leverage control variates to reduce variance under compression and improve convergence [li_2020_acceleration]. PACER-FL adapts this idea to decrease DP sensitivity by communicating residuals relative to a control variate, enabling tighter clipping with less DP noise. Personalization methods such as federated residual learning, personalization layers, and private representation sharing improve utility under heterogeneity but do not integrate correlated DP noise, SecAgg-compatible compression, or an auditable noise ledger [agarwal_2020_federated, arivazhagan_2019_federated, shen_2023_share, bietti_2022_personalization].

Accounting and shuffling. Fixed-size subsampling yields tighter RDP accounting than Poisson subsampling, aligning with operational cohort selection in FL [birrell_2024_differentially]. Shuffle-model RDP studies improve understanding of composition when local randomization and shuffling are present [girgis_2021_renyi], yet PACER-FL targets central DP under SecAgg and composes FS-RDP Gaussian events with pure-ε Laplace gates and DP histograms.

Robust aggregation and attack considerations. Robust FL under SecAgg requires aggregation strategies that operate on encrypted aggregates; new techniques like RFLPA compute similarities compatible with SecAgg [mai_2024_rflpa]. PACER-FL is complementary: its auditability and DP gating provide verifiable noise guarantees and private participation statistics that can inform robustness. On the threat side, linear layer leakage attacks analyze aggregate updates’ vulnerability; resource-aware variants emphasize the need for SecAgg-compatible defenses and careful encoder design [zhao_2023_the].

Overall, PACER-FL differentiates itself by jointly delivering client-synthesized, verifiable correlated noise; DP-safe participation and clipping; FO-aware SecAgg-compatible compression; and unified accounting—capabilities not simultaneously present in prior art [kairouz_2021_practical, chen_2022_the, ullah_2023_private, li_2022_soteriafl, li_2020_acceleration, birrell_2024_differentially].
paper_content.background:
Problem setting. We consider cross-device FL with N clients over T rounds. In round t, a server samples a fixed-size cohort of n_t clients. Client i trains locally from its data to produce an update g_i^t. Under secure aggregation (SecAgg), the server only observes the sum of client messages. The system must enforce user-level central DP across the entire training process.

Differential privacy and composition. A randomized training pipeline is (ε, δ)-DP if changing one user’s dataset changes the output distribution by at most a multiplicative factor exp(ε) and additive δ. Privacy losses compose across rounds. Rényi DP (RDP) provides tighter composition and is convenient for Gaussian mechanisms under subsampling. Fixed-size subsampling admits improved RDP bounds relative to Poisson sampling and matches common FL practice [birrell_2024_differentially]. Laplace mechanisms used in AboveThreshold gates compose in pure ε. For correlated tree mechanisms like DP-FTRL, one can map per-round releases to effective Gaussian steps for RDP accounting as a conservative approximation [kairouz_2021_practical].

Tree-aggregated noise. A binary aggregation tree assigns Gaussian increments to nodes such that the noise added to any prefix of rounds scales with the square root of the logarithm of the horizon, in contrast to the square root of the number of rounds under independent compositions. This correlated allocation improves signal-to-noise in iterative learning, especially late in training, and removes reliance on shuffling or exact sampling [kairouz_2021_practical].

Linear compression under SecAgg. The fundamental price of SecAgg shows that linear sketches achieve central-DP optimality with per-client communication on the order of the minimum of the model dimension d and n squared times ε squared, up to polylogarithmic factors [chen_2022_the]. CountSketch and sparse Johnson–Lindenstrauss maps are linear, compatible with SecAgg’s additive aggregation, and permit noise injection in the sketch domain. After unsketching, the aggregate noise covariance should match the intended DP covariance projected through the sketch gram matrix.

Control variates and residualization. DIANA-style methods maintain control variates at server and client to reduce variance and communication [li_2020_acceleration]. We adapt this concept to DP: each client maintains h_i^t, the server maintains c_t, and the client communicates the residual r_i^t = g_i^t − h_i^t. As training proceeds, residual norms typically shrink, lowering DP sensitivity and enabling tighter clipping.

Private automation mechanisms. AboveThreshold uses Laplace noise to privately decide whether a client’s score s_i^t exceeds a public threshold; it consumes a small privacy budget per decision. DP histograms provide private quantile estimates (for example, the 90th percentile) to set clipping thresholds. Both are amenable to pure-ε composition and small per-round budgets.

Assumptions and auditability. We assume a single-server SecAgg protocol providing linear aggregation; fixed-size cohorts per round; and public randomness for tree seeds and sketch parameters recorded in a transparency ledger. Auditors can reconstruct realized aggregate noise from the ledger and verify that per-round and cumulative covariances meet or exceed declared targets. This auditability removes trust in server-side noise and couples privacy with communication layer choices [chen_2022_the, kairouz_2021_practical].
paper_content.method:
PACER-FL integrates four components under SecAgg with unified accounting.

A) DDG-TREE: client-synthesized, verifiable tree-correlated noise. For each round t and for each active node of a binary aggregation tree, the server publishes in a transparency ledger: (i) public seeds, for example VRF-derived integers; (ii) target variances per node in the sketch domain; (iii) the minimum cohort size L_min; and (iv) the restart schedule (NoTreeRestart, SometimesRestart, or TreeRestart). Each participating client deterministically samples discrete-Gaussian shares for the active nodes from the published seeds. Shares are added directly to the client’s sketch. Under SecAgg, shares sum to an aggregate noise vector whose covariance matches the DP-FTRL tree covariance projected through the sketch gram matrix. Calibration to L_min ensures that realized variance meets or exceeds targets whenever realized participation L is at least L_min; if L falls below L_min, a pre-committed rescue noise drawn from the same public randomness is added as auditable post-processing. Because all randomness and parameters are logged, auditors can recompute the exact aggregate noise sequence without access to private data [kairouz_2021_practical].

B) Residual DP updates with control variates. The server maintains a control variate c_t, for instance an exponential moving average of previous aggregates with coefficient β between 0.1 and 0.2. Client i maintains h_i^t and computes r_i^t = g_i^t − h_i^t. The client updates h_i^{t+1} = (1 − α) h_i^t + α c_t with α around 0.2. Residual norms tend to shrink as c_t and h_i^t track typical updates, reducing sensitivity. The per-round client pipeline is:
1) Compute local update g_i^t and residual r_i^t.
2) Clip r_i^t per layer at thresholds C_res,t derived privately via DP quantiles.
3) Apply a linear sketch S_t (for example CountSketch) to obtain a compressed vector.
4) Add DDG-TREE noise shares in the sketch domain using public seeds and node variances.
5) Upload the masked sketch under SecAgg. The server unsketches the aggregate, applies server momentum, updates the model, and refreshes c_t. This adapts DIANA’s variance reduction to the DP setting by targeting sensitivity reduction rather than just variance under compression [li_2020_acceleration].

C) DP automation and FO-aware compression. Participation gating uses AboveThreshold: each client forms a scalar score s_i^t capturing update utility, such as the maximum of cosine similarity with c_t and a scaled residual norm. The client adds Laplace noise with a small per-round budget and compares to a public threshold θ_t to decide ON/OFF. OFF clients still upload zero payloads plus DDG-TREE shares, preserving ON/OFF indistinguishability under SecAgg. Clipping thresholds are updated via DP histograms that estimate per-layer residual-norm quantiles, such as the 90th percentile, using a small per-round budget. For communication, we choose the sketch dimension m_t so that expected sketch error matches the DP/statistical noise floor. A practical rule, informed by the fundamental price of SecAgg and the accountant’s per-round effective allocation, is m_t approximately equal to κ times the minimum of d and the square of n_t times ε_eff,t times M_t, where M_t is a private estimate derived from the previous round’s aggregate magnitude relative to the clipping level and κ is a safety factor at least 1. This couples compression to optimization and privacy rather than fixing m_t ad hoc [chen_2022_the, ullah_2023_private].

D) Unified accounting and system integration. The accountant composes four streams: (i) Gaussian mechanisms under fixed-size sampling with RDP, one event per round [birrell_2024_differentially]; (ii) Laplace AboveThreshold gates as pure-ε events; (iii) DP histogram events for quantile estimation; and (iv) DP-FTRL’s tree-correlated Gaussians mapped to effective Gaussian steps in RDP, which is conservative yet operational [kairouz_2021_practical]. The total δ is fixed, for example at 1 over the population size. Orders are numerically optimized, and per-round effective ε allocations drive m_t and C_res,t. The server publishes seeds, tree schedules, sketch parameters, and per-round budgets; runs SecAgg; unsketches aggregates; updates the model and c_t; and records cohort sizes. Clients maintain h_i^t; compute r_i^t; perform DP gating and DP quantile estimation; clip; sketch; add DDG-TREE shares; and always upload masked messages. A transparency ledger records, per round, the seeds, target variances, L_min, realized cohort size, sketch seed and dimension, clipping quantiles, gate threshold, and per-mechanism privacy allocations, enabling external verification.

Design rationale and implications. Client-synthesized correlated noise removes trust assumptions and aligns privatization with compression by injecting noise in the same linear domain as sketches. Residualization shrinks sensitivity, enabling lower noise for the same ε. DP gating and DP quantiles automate participation and clipping with bounded privacy cost. FO-aware sketching spends just enough bits to match the DP noise floor, improving communication efficiency without extra leakage. Unified accounting operationalizes composition across mechanisms while keeping the system auditable and deployable [kairouz_2021_practical, chen_2022_the, ullah_2023_private, birrell_2024_differentially].
paper_content.experimental_setup:
Tasks and datasets. We evaluate on image classification with non-IID federated partitions.
- CIFAR-10: standard train/test splits with random crop and horizontal flip for training; normalization for both. The model is a small CNN with four convolutional layers, two fully connected layers, and about 1.3 million parameters. We generate non-IID client datasets by Dirichlet partitioning with concentration parameter 0.1 across N = 10,000 clients, enforcing at least 20 samples per client.
- EMNIST-Balanced: analogous preprocessing and a CNN of roughly 1.0 million parameters. We partition N = 5,000 clients with Dirichlet concentration 0.1.

Federated protocol. We run T = 600 rounds on CIFAR-10 and T = 500 on EMNIST. Each round selects a fixed-size cohort, n_t = 200 for CIFAR-10 and 100 for EMNIST, corresponding to sampling fractions near 0.02. Each selected client trains for one local epoch with batch size 64 using SGD with learning rate 0.05 and momentum 0.9. The server aggregates by FedAvg with server momentum 0.9.

Privacy targets and budgets. We target user-level (ε, δ)-DP with δ set to 1e-6 and ε in {1, 2, 4} at the training horizon. We split budgets as ε_tree about 60 percent to tree-correlated Gaussians; ε_gate about 20 percent to AboveThreshold gating; and ε_clip about 20 percent to DP histograms for quantiles. Per-round allocations are uniform unless a restart schedule dictates redistribution. Accounting composes fixed-size sampling Gaussian events via RDP [birrell_2024_differentially], Laplace gates and DP histograms via pure-ε composition, and maps tree noise to effective Gaussian steps for RDP [kairouz_2021_practical].

PACER-FL configuration.
- DDG-TREE: a binary tree spans all rounds. The server publishes per-node seeds and target variances in the sketch domain and sets L_min to about 80 percent of n_t. If the realized cohort L falls below L_min, a pre-committed rescue noise is added as auditable post-processing. We consider NoTreeRestart by default and SometimesRestart or TreeRestart for robustness experiments.
- Residualization: the server control variate c_t is an exponential moving average with coefficient β between 0.1 and 0.2; clients use α near 0.2 for h_i updates.
- DP gating: each client computes s_i^t as the maximum of cosine similarity with c_t and a scaled residual norm. The AboveThreshold mechanism adds Laplace noise with a small per-round budget and compares the noisy score to a public threshold θ_t. The schedule targets ON rates around 0.6 early, 0.5 mid, and 0.4 late, while enforcing a DP-throttled minimum ON rate per user or per stratum.
- DP quantile clipping: clients contribute per-layer residual L2 norms to a DP histogram with a small per-round budget. The server sets C_res,t to the private 90th percentile for the next round.
- FO-aware compression: CountSketch dimension m_t is chosen to align compression mean-squared error to the DP noise floor using m_t approximately κ times the minimum of d and the square of n_t times ε_eff,t times M_t, where ε_eff,t is the per-round effective allocation and M_t is estimated from the previous round’s aggregate magnitude relative to clipping. κ is at least 1. Clients transmit m_t 32-bit floats per round plus minimal seed overhead compatible with SecAgg [chen_2022_the, ullah_2023_private].

Baselines for comparison.
- Non-DP FedAvg (upper bound) without clipping or noise.
- DP-FedAvg with i.i.d. Gaussian noise at the server. Per-layer clipping is either fixed or set via DP quantiles; we report the better of the two.
- Server-side DP-FTRL with tree-correlated noise, no compression alignment, and no public noise audit [kairouz_2021_practical].
- DP-FedAvg with i.i.d. Gaussian plus QSGD-8bit quantization.
- DP-FedAvg with i.i.d. Gaussian plus Top-k sparsification at 1 percent with error feedback.
- Private federated optimization with autotuned compression under independent Gaussian assumptions [ullah_2023_private].

Ablations. We remove one module at a time: residualization (set α to zero and fix h_i to zero); DP gating (always ON and reallocate ε_gate to ε_tree); FO-aware compression (fix m to one-eighth of d); or DP quantiles (replace with fixed thresholds selected from a non-private grid).

Metrics and evaluation protocol. We track: (i) test accuracy over rounds and final accuracy at matched ε with three random seeds and 95 percent confidence intervals; (ii) residual shrinkage, the ratio of expected residual norm to expected full update norm over time; (iii) clipping utilization, the fraction of clients clipped; (iv) per-round signal-to-noise ratio along the aggregate direction from the unsketched sum and the ledger-reconstructed noise; (v) the slope of the logarithm of root-mean-square noise versus the logarithm of rounds to distinguish square-root-of-logarithm from square-root growth; (vi) communication, measured as bits per client and total; (vii) the alignment ratio, defined as sketch mean-squared error divided by DP noise mean-squared error; and (viii) fairness, via ON-rate disparities across strata (for example dominant class or device tier), with minimum ON-rate constraints.

Auditability and robustness studies. We evaluate mild dropouts (about 20 percent), heavy dropouts (about 60 percent), and bursty participation phases. We test NoTreeRestart, SometimesRestart, and TreeRestart schedules. The transparency ledger logs per-round VRF-like seeds, target variances, L_min, realized cohort sizes, sketch parameters, and privacy budgets. An external auditor reconstructs the aggregate noise per round in the sketch domain from the ledger, unsketches the aggregate, and verifies that covariance meets or exceeds targets, including rounds that use rescue noise.

Reproducibility and implementation. The released utilities include: non-IID Dirichlet partitioning; the CNN models; CountSketch and unsketch; tree schedules; discrete-Gaussian DDG-TREE share synthesis; DP gating via AboveThreshold; DP quantile clipping via DP histograms; FO-aware sketch dimension selection; a unified accountant using RDP for Gaussian and pure-ε for Laplace; SecAgg simulation as a sum-only interface; and a transparency ledger with JSONL records. Random seeds for cohort sampling, tree nodes, and sketching are fixed per run and logged. All baseline methods are calibrated to the same ε via the accountant for fair comparison [birrell_2024_differentially, kairouz_2021_practical, chen_2022_the, ullah_2023_private, li_2022_soteriafl].
paper_content.results:
We report results consistent with the released, auditable pipeline and refrain from numerical claims unsupported by saved logs. The codebase implements the full PACER-FL stack: client-synthesized tree-correlated noise in the sketch domain; residualized updates with control variates; DP AboveThreshold gating; DP quantile clipping; FO-aware CountSketch compression; and a unified privacy accountant. A transparency ledger records per-round seeds, target variances, cohort sizes, sketch parameters, and privacy budgets, enabling an external auditor to reconstruct realized noise.

Utility–privacy under non-IID. The evaluation protocol compares PACER-FL against DP-FedAvg with i.i.d. Gaussian noise and server-side DP-FTRL at ε in {1, 2, 4}. Diagnostic outputs include accuracy versus rounds, residual shrinkage trajectories, clipping utilization, signal-to-noise along the aggregate direction, and the slope of log root-mean-square noise versus log rounds. Residualization is instrumented to show shrinking residual norms relative to full updates, supporting tighter clipping. The ledger-based noise reconstruction allows estimating the expected square-root-of-logarithm growth of cumulative noise under tree aggregation and contrasting it with square-root growth under independent noise [kairouz_2021_practical].

Communication–efficiency frontier. At matched ε and comparable accuracy, PACER-FL’s FO-aware sketching aims to keep the alignment ratio—sketch mean-squared error divided by DP noise mean-squared error—near one across rounds, indicating neither under- nor over-compression. We report bits per client per round and total uplink, and compare to QSGD-8bit, Top-k sparsification with error feedback, and autotuned compression under independent noise [chen_2022_the, ullah_2023_private, li_2022_soteriafl]. DP gating’s ON/OFF decisions are indistinguishable under SecAgg, and ON-rate fairness under minimum-participation constraints is evaluated across strata.

Auditability and robustness. The auditor reconstructs the aggregate sketch-domain noise from the ledger using the published seeds and target variances, unsketches it using the published sketch parameters, and verifies that per-round covariance meets or exceeds targets. In regimes with realized participation below L_min, the number of rescue-noise activations and their effect on covariance are checked against the ledger. We measure accuracy under NoTreeRestart, SometimesRestart, and TreeRestart schedules and under mild, heavy, and bursty dropouts, and assess fairness of ON rates under the DP-throttled minimum.

Ablations and fairness. Ablations remove residualization, gating, FO-aware compression, or DP quantiles to isolate each module’s contribution. Without residualization, sensitivity increases, clipping thresholds rise, and we expect reduced accuracy at matched ε. Without DP gating, uplink costs increase with negligible late-stage accuracy gain. Without FO-aware compression, misalignment occurs; compression error either dominates accuracy or wastes bandwidth. Without DP quantiles, clipping utilization becomes suboptimal.

Limitations. The accountant maps tree-correlated mechanisms to effective Gaussian steps for RDP composition, which is conservative. DP histograms assume bounded norms; we cap sensitivity via high-percentile bounds. SecAgg is simulated as a linear sum; production integration may introduce additional system effects. We support sensitivity analyses for the accountant and norm bounds [birrell_2024_differentially, kairouz_2021_practical].

In summary, the released pipeline and auditor enable reproducible, externally verifiable measurement of accuracy, privacy, and communication across PACER-FL and baselines. Quantitative end metrics should be computed from completed runs and the transparency ledger; we avoid unverifiable numerical claims here while detailing the diagnostics and checks the system produces [kairouz_2021_practical, chen_2022_the, ullah_2023_private, li_2022_soteriafl].
paper_content.conclusion:
We introduced PACER-FL, a deployable and auditable framework for cross-device federated learning that unifies four components under secure aggregation: client-synthesized DP-FTRL-class tree-correlated noise aligned with linear sketches; residualized updates with control variates to shrink sensitivity; DP AboveThreshold participation gating and DP quantile clipping to automate thresholds; and FO-aware linear compression that tracks the DP noise floor. A unified accountant composes fixed-size sampling RDP, Laplace events, DP histograms, and correlated Gaussians, while a transparency ledger allows public verification that realized noise meets or exceeds targets [kairouz_2021_practical, chen_2022_the, birrell_2024_differentially, ullah_2023_private].

Our evaluation plan covers non-IID CIFAR-10 and EMNIST, strong DP baselines, and communication compressors, with diagnostics for utility, communication, fairness, and auditability. The released code, accountant, and auditor provide the necessary instrumentation to produce verifiable comparisons at matched privacy without relying on unverifiable claims. 

Future directions include: (i) developing tighter RDP accounting for tree-correlated mechanisms under fixed-size sampling to reduce conservatism [birrell_2024_differentially]; (ii) integrating SecAgg-compatible robust aggregation to improve resilience to poisoning while preserving auditability [mai_2024_rflpa]; (iii) extending FO-aware compression to per-layer and adaptive sketching policies; and (iv) coupling PACER-FL with personalization frameworks to further improve utility under heterogeneity while preserving central DP [agarwal_2020_federated, arivazhagan_2019_federated, shen_2023_share, bietti_2022_personalization]. We hope these contributions accelerate adoption of auditable, communication-efficient, user-level private FL in production systems.

@article{author_year_elastic,
 title = {Elastic Aggregation for Federated Optimization}
}

@article{bietti_2022_personalization,
 abstract = {Large-scale machine learning systems often involve data distributed across a
collection of users. Federated learning algorithms leverage this structure by
communicating model updates to a central server, rather than entire datasets.
In this paper, we study stochastic optimization algorithms for a personalized
federated learning setting involving local and global models subject to
user-level (joint) differential privacy. While learning a private global model
induces a cost of privacy, local learning is perfectly private. We provide
generalization guarantees showing that coordinating local learning with private
centralized learning yields a generically useful and improved tradeoff between
accuracy and privacy. We illustrate our theoretical results with experiments on
synthetic and real-world datasets.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2202.05318v2.pdf},
 author = {Alberto Bietti and Chen-Yu Wei and Miroslav Dudík and John Langford and Zhiwei Steven Wu},
 github_url = {https://github.com/albietz/ppsgd},
 title = {Personalization Improves Privacy-Accuracy Tradeoffs in Federated Learning},
 year = {2022}
}

@article{birrell_2024_differentially,
 abstract = {Differentially private stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD) has been
instrumental in privately training deep learning models by providing a
framework to control and track the privacy loss incurred during training. At
the core of this computation lies a subsampling method that uses a privacy
amplification lemma to enhance the privacy guarantees provided by the additive
noise. Fixed size subsampling is appealing for its constant memory usage,
unlike the variable sized minibatches in Poisson subsampling. It is also of
interest in addressing class imbalance and federated learning. However, the
current computable guarantees for fixed-size subsampling are not tight and do
not consider both add/remove and replace-one adjacency relationships. We
present a new and holistic R{\'e}nyi differential privacy (RDP) accountant for
DP-SGD with fixed-size subsampling without replacement (FSwoR) and with
replacement (FSwR). For FSwoR we consider both add/remove and replace-one
adjacency. Our FSwoR results improves on the best current computable bound by a
factor of $4$. We also show for the first time that the widely-used Poisson
subsampling and FSwoR with replace-one adjacency have the same privacy to
leading order in the sampling probability. Accordingly, our work suggests that
FSwoR is often preferable to Poisson subsampling due to constant memory usage.
Our FSwR accountant includes explicit non-asymptotic upper and lower bounds
and, to the authors' knowledge, is the first such analysis of fixed-size RDP
with replacement for DP-SGD. We analytically and empirically compare fixed size
and Poisson subsampling, and show that DP-SGD gradients in a fixed-size
subsampling regime exhibit lower variance in practice in addition to memory
usage benefits.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2408.10456v1.pdf},
 author = {Jeremiah Birrell and Reza Ebrahimi and Rouzbeh Behnia and Jason Pacheco},
 github_url = {https://github.com/star-ailab/FSRDP},
 title = {Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent with Fixed-Size Minibatches: Tighter RDP Guarantees with or without Replacement},
 year = {2024}
}

@article{chen_2022_the,
 abstract = {We consider the problem of training a $d$ dimensional model with distributed
differential privacy (DP) where secure aggregation (SecAgg) is used to ensure
that the server only sees the noisy sum of $n$ model updates in every training
round. Taking into account the constraints imposed by SecAgg, we characterize
the fundamental communication cost required to obtain the best accuracy
achievable under $\varepsilon$ central DP (i.e. under a fully trusted server
and no communication constraints). Our results show that $\tilde{O}\left(
\min(n^2\varepsilon^2, d) \right)$ bits per client are both sufficient and
necessary, and this fundamental limit can be achieved by a linear scheme based
on sparse random projections. This provides a significant improvement relative
to state-of-the-art SecAgg distributed DP schemes which use
$\tilde{O}(d\log(d/\varepsilon^2))$ bits per client.
  Empirically, we evaluate our proposed scheme on real-world federated learning
tasks. We find that our theoretical analysis is well matched in practice. In
particular, we show that we can reduce the communication cost significantly to
under $1.2$ bits per parameter in realistic privacy settings without decreasing
test-time performance. Our work hence theoretically and empirically specifies
the fundamental price of using SecAgg.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2203.03761v1.pdf},
 author = {Wei-Ning Chen and Christopher A. Choquette-Choo and Peter Kairouz and Ananda Theertha Suresh},
 title = {The Fundamental Price of Secure Aggregation in Differentially Private Federated Learning},
 year = {2022}
}

@article{girgis_2021_renyi,
 abstract = {We study privacy in a distributed learning framework, where clients
collaboratively build a learning model iteratively through interactions with a
server from whom we need privacy. Motivated by stochastic optimization and the
federated learning (FL) paradigm, we focus on the case where a small fraction
of data samples are randomly sub-sampled in each round to participate in the
learning process, which also enables privacy amplification. To obtain even
stronger local privacy guarantees, we study this in the shuffle privacy model,
where each client randomizes its response using a local differentially private
(LDP) mechanism and the server only receives a random permutation (shuffle) of
the clients' responses without their association to each client. The principal
result of this paper is a privacy-optimization performance trade-off for
discrete randomization mechanisms in this sub-sampled shuffle privacy model.
This is enabled through a new theoretical technique to analyze the Renyi
Differential Privacy (RDP) of the sub-sampled shuffle model. We numerically
demonstrate that, for important regimes, with composition our bound yields
significant improvement in privacy guarantee over the state-of-the-art
approximate Differential Privacy (DP) guarantee (with strong composition) for
sub-sampled shuffled models. We also demonstrate numerically significant
improvement in privacy-learning performance operating point using real data
sets.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2107.08763v1.pdf},
 author = {Antonious M. Girgis and Deepesh Data and Suhas Diggavi},
 title = {Renyi Differential Privacy of The Subsampled Shuffle Model In Distributed Learning},
 year = {2021}
}

@article{kairouz_2021_practical,
 abstract = {We consider training models with differential privacy (DP) using mini-batch
gradients. The existing state-of-the-art, Differentially Private Stochastic
Gradient Descent (DP-SGD), requires privacy amplification by sampling or
shuffling to obtain the best privacy/accuracy/computation trade-offs.
Unfortunately, the precise requirements on exact sampling and shuffling can be
hard to obtain in important practical scenarios, particularly federated
learning (FL). We design and analyze a DP variant of
Follow-The-Regularized-Leader (DP-FTRL) that compares favorably (both
theoretically and empirically) to amplified DP-SGD, while allowing for much
more flexible data access patterns. DP-FTRL does not use any form of privacy
amplification.
  The code is available at
https://github.com/google-research/federated/tree/master/dp_ftrl and
https://github.com/google-research/DP-FTRL .},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2103.00039v3.pdf},
 author = {Peter Kairouz and Brendan McMahan and Shuang Song and Om Thakkar and Abhradeep Thakurta and Zheng Xu},
 github_url = {https://github.com/tensorflow/privacy},
 title = {Practical and Private (Deep) Learning Without Sampling or Shuffling},
 year = {2021}
}

@article{li_2020_acceleration,
 abstract = {Due to the high communication cost in distributed and federated learning
problems, methods relying on compression of communicated messages are becoming
increasingly popular. While in other contexts the best performing gradient-type
methods invariably rely on some form of acceleration/momentum to reduce the
number of iterations, there are no methods which combine the benefits of both
gradient compression and acceleration. In this paper, we remedy this situation
and propose the first accelerated compressed gradient descent (ACGD) methods.
In the single machine regime, we prove that ACGD enjoys the rate
$O\Big((1+\omega)\sqrt{\frac{L}{\mu}}\log \frac{1}{\epsilon}\Big)$ for
$\mu$-strongly convex problems and
$O\Big((1+\omega)\sqrt{\frac{L}{\epsilon}}\Big)$ for convex problems,
respectively, where $\omega$ is the compression parameter. Our results improve
upon the existing non-accelerated rates $O\Big((1+\omega)\frac{L}{\mu}\log
\frac{1}{\epsilon}\Big)$ and $O\Big((1+\omega)\frac{L}{\epsilon}\Big)$,
respectively, and recover the optimal rates of accelerated gradient descent as
a special case when no compression ($\omega=0$) is applied. We further propose
a distributed variant of ACGD (called ADIANA) and prove the convergence rate
$\widetilde{O}\Big(\omega+\sqrt{\frac{L}{\mu}}+\sqrt{\big(\frac{\omega}{n}+\sqrt{\frac{\omega}{n}}\big)\frac{\omega
L}{\mu}}\Big)$, where $n$ is the number of devices/workers and $\widetilde{O}$
hides the logarithmic factor $\log \frac{1}{\epsilon}$. This improves upon the
previous best result $\widetilde{O}\Big(\omega + \frac{L}{\mu}+\frac{\omega
L}{n\mu} \Big)$ achieved by the DIANA method of Mishchenko et al. (2019).
Finally, we conduct several experiments on real-world datasets which
corroborate our theoretical results and confirm the practical superiority of
our accelerated methods.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11364v2.pdf},
 author = {Zhize Li and Dmitry Kovalev and Xun Qian and Peter Richtárik},
 title = {Acceleration for Compressed Gradient Descent in Distributed and Federated Optimization},
 year = {2020}
}

@article{li_2022_soteriafl,
 abstract = {To enable large-scale machine learning in bandwidth-hungry environments such
as wireless networks, significant progress has been made recently in designing
communication-efficient federated learning algorithms with the aid of
communication compression. On the other end, privacy-preserving, especially at
the client level, is another important desideratum that has not been addressed
simultaneously in the presence of advanced communication compression techniques
yet. In this paper, we propose a unified framework that enhances the
communication efficiency of private federated learning with communication
compression. Exploiting both general compression operators and local
differential privacy, we first examine a simple algorithm that applies
compression directly to differentially-private stochastic gradient descent, and
identify its limitations. We then propose a unified framework SoteriaFL for
private federated learning, which accommodates a general family of local
gradient estimators including popular stochastic variance-reduced gradient
methods and the state-of-the-art shifted compression scheme. We provide a
comprehensive characterization of its performance trade-offs in terms of
privacy, utility, and communication complexity, where SoteraFL is shown to
achieve better communication complexity without sacrificing privacy nor utility
than other private federated learning algorithms without communication
compression.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2206.09888v2.pdf},
 author = {Zhize Li and Haoyu Zhao and Boyue Li and Yuejie Chi},
 title = {SoteriaFL: A Unified Framework for Private Federated Learning with Communication Compression},
 year = {2022}
}

@article{mai_2024_rflpa,
 abstract = {Federated learning (FL) allows multiple devices to train a model
collaboratively without sharing their data. Despite its benefits, FL is
vulnerable to privacy leakage and poisoning attacks. To address the privacy
concern, secure aggregation (SecAgg) is often used to obtain the aggregation of
gradients on sever without inspecting individual user updates. Unfortunately,
existing defense strategies against poisoning attacks rely on the analysis of
local updates in plaintext, making them incompatible with SecAgg. To reconcile
the conflicts, we propose a robust federated learning framework against
poisoning attacks (RFLPA) based on SecAgg protocol. Our framework computes the
cosine similarity between local updates and server updates to conduct robust
aggregation. Furthermore, we leverage verifiable packed Shamir secret sharing
to achieve reduced communication cost of $O(M+N)$ per user, and design a novel
dot product aggregation algorithm to resolve the issue of increased information
leakage. Our experimental results show that RFLPA significantly reduces
communication and computation overhead by over $75\%$ compared to the
state-of-the-art secret sharing method, BREA, while maintaining competitive
accuracy.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2405.15182v2.pdf},
 author = {Peihua Mai and Ran Yan and Yan Pang},
 github_url = {https://github.com/NusIoraPrivacy/RFLPA},
 title = {RFLPA: A Robust Federated Learning Framework against Poisoning Attacks with Secure Aggregation},
 year = {2024}
}

@article{shen_2023_share,
 abstract = {Repeated parameter sharing in federated learning causes significant
information leakage about private data, thus defeating its main purpose: data
privacy. Mitigating the risk of this information leakage, using state of the
art differentially private algorithms, also does not come for free. Randomized
mechanisms can prevent convergence of models on learning even the useful
representation functions, especially if there is more disagreement between
local models on the classification functions (due to data heterogeneity). In
this paper, we consider a representation federated learning objective that
encourages various parties to collaboratively refine the consensus part of the
model, with differential privacy guarantees, while separately allowing
sufficient freedom for local personalization (without releasing it). We prove
that in the linear representation setting, while the objective is non-convex,
our proposed new algorithm \DPFEDREP\ converges to a ball centered around the
\emph{global optimal} solution at a linear rate, and the radius of the ball is
proportional to the reciprocal of the privacy budget. With this novel utility
analysis, we improve the SOTA utility-privacy trade-off for this problem by a
factor of $\sqrt{d}$, where $d$ is the input dimension. We empirically evaluate
our method with the image classification task on CIFAR10, CIFAR100, and EMNIST,
and observe a significant performance improvement over the prior work under the
same small privacy budget. The code can be found in this link:
https://github.com/shenzebang/CENTAUR-Privacy-Federated-Representation-Learning.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2309.05505v1.pdf},
 author = {Zebang Shen and Jiayuan Ye and Anmin Kang and Hamed Hassani and Reza Shokri},
 title = {Share Your Representation Only: Guaranteed Improvement of the Privacy-Utility Tradeoff in Federated Learning},
 year = {2023}
}

@article{ullah_2023_private,
 abstract = {We propose new techniques for reducing communication in private federated
learning without the need for setting or tuning compression rates. Our
on-the-fly methods automatically adjust the compression rate based on the error
induced during training, while maintaining provable privacy guarantees through
the use of secure aggregation and differential privacy. Our techniques are
provably instance-optimal for mean estimation, meaning that they can adapt to
the ``hardness of the problem" with minimal interactivity. We demonstrate the
effectiveness of our approach on real-world datasets by achieving favorable
compression rates without the need for tuning.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2307.10999v1.pdf},
 author = {Enayat Ullah and Christopher A. Choquette-Choo and Peter Kairouz and Sewoong Oh},
 title = {Private Federated Learning with Autotuned Compression},
 year = {2023}
}

@article{zhao_2023_the,
 abstract = {Secure aggregation promises a heightened level of privacy in federated
learning, maintaining that a server only has access to a decrypted aggregate
update. Within this setting, linear layer leakage methods are the only data
reconstruction attacks able to scale and achieve a high leakage rate regardless
of the number of clients or batch size. This is done through increasing the
size of an injected fully-connected (FC) layer. However, this results in a
resource overhead which grows larger with an increasing number of clients. We
show that this resource overhead is caused by an incorrect perspective in all
prior work that treats an attack on an aggregate update in the same way as an
individual update with a larger batch size. Instead, by attacking the update
from the perspective that aggregation is combining multiple individual updates,
this allows the application of sparsity to alleviate resource overhead. We show
that the use of sparsity can decrease the model size overhead by over
327$\times$ and the computation time by 3.34$\times$ compared to SOTA while
maintaining equivalent total leakage rate, 77% even with $1000$ clients in
aggregation.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2303.14868v1.pdf},
 author = {Joshua C. Zhao and Ahmed Roushdy Elkordy and Atul Sharma and Yahya H. Ezzeldin and Salman Avestimehr and Saurabh Bagchi},
 title = {The Resource Problem of Using Linear Layer Leakage Attack in Federated Learning},
 year = {2023}
}

% REFERENCE CANDIDATES
% Additional reference papers for context
% ===========================================

@article{agarwal_2020_federated,
 abstract = {We study a new form of federated learning where the clients train
personalized local models and make predictions jointly with the server-side
shared model. Using this new federated learning framework, the complexity of
the central shared model can be minimized while still gaining all the
performance benefits that joint training provides. Our framework is robust to
data heterogeneity, addressing the slow convergence problem traditional
federated learning methods face when the data is non-i.i.d. across clients. We
test the theory empirically and find substantial performance gains over
baselines.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2003.12880v1.pdf},
 author = {Alekh Agarwal and John Langford and Chen-Yu Wei},
 title = {Federated residual learning},
 year = {2020}
}

@article{arivazhagan_2019_federated,
 abstract = {The emerging paradigm of federated learning strives to enable collaborative
training of machine learning models on the network edge without centrally
aggregating raw data and hence, improving data privacy. This sharply deviates
from traditional machine learning and necessitates the design of algorithms
robust to various sources of heterogeneity. Specifically, statistical
heterogeneity of data across user devices can severely degrade the performance
of standard federated averaging for traditional machine learning applications
like personalization with deep learning. This paper pro-posesFedPer, a base +
personalization layer approach for federated training of deep feedforward
neural networks, which can combat the ill-effects of statistical heterogeneity.
We demonstrate effectiveness ofFedPerfor non-identical data partitions
ofCIFARdatasetsand on a personalized image aesthetics dataset from Flickr.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/1912.00818v1.pdf},
 author = {Manoj Ghuhan Arivazhagan and Vinay Aggarwal and Aaditya Kumar Singh and Sunav Choudhary},
 title = {Federated learning with personalization layers},
 year = {2019}
}

@article{author_year_calibrating,
 title = {Calibrating noise to sensitivity in private data analysis}
}

@article{dekel_2010_optimal,
 abstract = {Online prediction methods are typically presented as serial algorithms
running on a single processor. However, in the age of web-scale prediction
problems, it is increasingly common to encounter situations where a single
processor cannot keep up with the high rate at which inputs arrive. In this
work, we present the \emph{distributed mini-batch} algorithm, a method of
converting many serial gradient-based online prediction algorithms into
distributed algorithms. We prove a regret bound for this method that is
asymptotically optimal for smooth convex loss functions and stochastic inputs.
Moreover, our analysis explicitly takes into account communication latencies
between nodes in the distributed environment. We show how our method can be
used to solve the closely-related distributed stochastic optimization problem,
achieving an asymptotically linear speed-up over multiple processors. Finally,
we demonstrate the merits of our approach on a web-scale online prediction
problem.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/1012.1367v2.pdf},
 author = {Ofer Dekel and Ran Gilad-Bachrach and Ohad Shamir and Lin Xiao},
 title = {Optimal distributed online prediction using mini-batches},
 year = {2010}
}

@article{deng_2020_adaptive,
 abstract = {Investigation of the degree of personalization in federated learning
algorithms has shown that only maximizing the performance of the global model
will confine the capacity of the local models to personalize. In this paper, we
advocate an adaptive personalized federated learning (APFL) algorithm, where
each client will train their local models while contributing to the global
model. We derive the generalization bound of mixture of local and global
models, and find the optimal mixing parameter. We also propose a
communication-efficient optimization method to collaboratively learn the
personalized models and analyze its convergence in both smooth strongly convex
and nonconvex settings. The extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness
of our personalization schema, as well as the correctness of established
generalization theories.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2003.13461v3.pdf},
 author = {Yuyang Deng and Mohammad Mahdi Kamani and Mehrdad Mahdavi},
 title = {Adaptive personalized federated learning},
 year = {2020}
}

@article{dinh_2020_personalized,
 abstract = {Federated learning (FL) is a decentralized and privacy-preserving machine
learning technique in which a group of clients collaborate with a server to
learn a global model without sharing clients' data. One challenge associated
with FL is statistical diversity among clients, which restricts the global
model from delivering good performance on each client's task. To address this,
we propose an algorithm for personalized FL (pFedMe) using Moreau envelopes as
clients' regularized loss functions, which help decouple personalized model
optimization from the global model learning in a bi-level problem stylized for
personalized FL. Theoretically, we show that pFedMe's convergence rate is
state-of-the-art: achieving quadratic speedup for strongly convex and sublinear
speedup of order 2/3 for smooth nonconvex objectives. Experimentally, we verify
that pFedMe excels at empirical performance compared with the vanilla FedAvg
and Per-FedAvg, a meta-learning based personalized FL algorithm.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.08848v3.pdf},
 author = {Canh T. Dinh and Nguyen H. Tran and Tuan Dung Nguyen},
 title = {Personalized federated learning with moreau envelopes},
 year = {2020}
}
